Dr Bill Ellis Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors – Diagonalisation

Aims Revision of Linear Algebra

1. Diagonalise a matrix (when possible) by using its Warning eigenvalues and eigenvectors. I We start with a quick review of key topics from first year 2. Diagonalise a real symmetric matrix using its eigenvalues linear algebra: matrices, inverses, determinants, systems and orthonormal eigenvectors. of linear equations, Gaussian Elimination and row reduction. 3. Apply matrix diagonalisation to the study of quadratic I If you are not confident with this material you should look surfaces and systems of ODEs. over your first year linear algebra notes.

Definition 4 I We say that an n × m matrix B = [bij ] is the transpose of an m × n matrix A = [aij ] if

bij = aji for 1 ≤ i ≤ n and 1 ≤ j ≤ m.

In this case, we put A⊤ = B. I The ith row of A⊤ has the same entries as the ith column of A. I The ith column of A⊤ has the same entries as the ith row of A. I A matrix A is said to be symmetric if A = A⊤ .

det(A) = −2 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors – Diagonalisation Definition 8 Given a square matrix A, a non-zero vector ⃗v is said to be an eigenvector of A if

A⃗v = λ⃗v for some λ ∈ C. (4)

The number λ is called the eigenvalue of A associated with ⃗v . Notes I An eigenvector cannot be the zero vector, but an       eigenvalue can be zero. x 2 − 32 − 12 1  y  = 4 , I Zero is an eigenvalue of A if and only if A is singular (or A−1 =  12 1 0 z 5 2 0 −1 not invertible) because A⃗v = ⃗0 and ⃗v 6= ⃗0. How can we find eigenvalues and eigenvectors? Trace of a matrix The trace of an n × n matrix A = [aij ] is the sum of its diagonal entries, that is, I Equation (4) can be rewritten as Xn Tr(A) = aii = a11 + a22 + · · · + ann . (A − λI)⃗v = ⃗0. i=1

The trace is a linear operator from Rn×n to R, that is, Since ⃗v 6= ⃗0, the matrix A − λI is not invertible, that is, det(A − λI) = 0. Tr(A + B) = Tr(A) + Tr(B) and Tr(αA) = α Tr(A) I Hence for A, B ∈ Rn×n and α ∈ R. I We find the eigenvalues by factorising the characteristic Later, we will show that polynomial det(λI − A) = (λ − λ1 )(λ − λ2 ) · · · (λ − λn ). X n X n

I Having found the λ’s, we find the ⃗v ’s by solving, for Tr(AB) = aij bji = Tr(BA), i=1 j=1 each λ, the system of linear equations (A − λI)⃗v = ⃗0, using row reduction and back substitution. and also that if λ1 , λ2 , …, λn are the eigenvalues of A, then X n Tr(A) = λi = λ1 + λ2 + · · · + λ n . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . i=1 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .  Eigenvalues and eigenvectors of inverse matrices Linear independence of eigenvectors Lemma 10 If A is an invertible matrix with eigenvector ⃗v and The eigenvectors associated with distinct eigenvalues of a corresponding eigenvalue λ, then ⃗v is also an eigenvector of matrix A are linearly independent. A−1 and the corresponding eigenvalue is 1/λ. (Why can’t λ = 0?) Proof. Assume that Proof. A⃗v = λ⃗v , Aw ⃗ = µw, ⃗ λ 6= µ, ⃗v 6= ⃗0, ⃗ 6= ⃗0. w

To show that ⃗v and w⃗ are linearly independent, suppose ⃗ = ⃗0. Then, α⃗v + β w ⃗0 = (A − λI)(α⃗v + β w) ⃗ = β(µ − λ)w, ⃗

implying that β(µ − λ) = 0. By assumption, µ − λ 6= 0 so β = 0. Similarly, using A − µI yields α = 0. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

Then, I Since eigenvectors associated with distinct eigenvalues are AP = [A⃗v1 ··· A⃗vn ] = [λ1⃗v1 ··· λn⃗vn ] = P D, linearly independent, if an n × n matrix A has n distinct eigenvalues, then A can be diagonalised. and so P −1 AP = D. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . Example 16 Let   5 −4 4 A = 12 −11 12 . 4 −4 5

1. Without finding the characteristic polynomial det(A − λI), verify that A has eigenvalues λ1 = −3 and λ2 = λ3 = 1, 2. Find corresponding eigenvectors. 3. Show that A can be diagonalised.

. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

I The above two examples show that if a matrix A has repeated eigenvalues, it might or might not be diagonalisable. I Next, we will discuss the special case when A is a real symmetric matrix. Fortunately, such a matrix can always be diagonalised, regardless of whether or not it has repeated eigenvalues. I Moreover, for such a matrix A, we can find eigenvectors that are orthogonal. We first define this concept.

  1 1 −1 P = 3 1 0  1 0 1 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .  Diagonalisation of a Real Symmetric Matrix Example 18 Definition 17 Show the vectors The set {⃗v1 , . . . , ⃗vm } of vectors in Rn is said to be orthogonal       if 1 −2 1 1   1 1 ⃗v1 = √ 1 , ⃗v2 = √  0  , ⃗v3 = √ −5 , ⃗vi · ⃗vj = ⃗vi⊤⃗vj = 0 whenever i 6= j with i, j ∈ {1, 2, . . . , m}. 6 2 5 1 30 2

The set is said to be orthonormal if it is orthogonal and form an orthonormal set.

k⃗vi k = 1 for i = 1, . . . , m.

Note An orthogonal set of m non-zero vectors must be linearly independent in Rn , and hence m ≤ n must hold.

. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

Orthogonal matrix

I A square matrix Q is said to be orthogonal if
